sources.code:
# Time:  O(n)
# Space: O(1)

class Solution(object):
    # @param A, a list of integers
    # @return an integer
    def jump(self, A):
        jump_count = 0
        reachable = 0
        curr_reachable = 0
        for i, length in enumerate(A):
            if i > reachable:
                return -1
            if i > curr_reachable:
                curr_reachable = reachable
                jump_count += 1
            reachable = max(reachable, i + length)
        return jump_count
sources.originalCode:
# Time:  O(n)
# Space: O(1)

class Solution(object):
    # @param A, a list of integers
    # @return an integer
    def jump(self, A):
        jump_count = 0
        reachable = 0
        curr_reachable = 0
        for i, length in enumerate(A):
            if i > reachable:
                return -1
            if i > curr_reachable:
                curr_reachable = reachable
                jump_count += 1
            reachable = max(reachable, i + length)
        return jump_count
sources.reviewedCode:
# Time:  O(n)
# Space: O(1)

# [Reader note] Entry point
# Start from the core routine and keep this mental model in view: Think in terms of how far the current wave of reachable positions can take you before another jump becomes unavoidable.
class Solution(object):
    # @param A, a list of integers
    # @return an integer
    def jump(self, A):
        # [Reader note] State setup
        # This setup stage creates the state needed for the invariant or partial result: Range-based state thinking, measuring progress in layers or intervals, and comparing constructive approaches.
        jump_count = 0
        reachable = 0
        curr_reachable = 0
        # [Reader note] Main pass
        # This is the main sweep where the implementation earns its efficiency instead of restarting work from scratch.
        for i, length in enumerate(A):
            # [Reader note] Decision point
            # Pay attention here: It is easy to focus on individual jumps when the cleaner viewpoint is to reason about the current reachable band.
            if i > reachable:
                return -1
            if i > curr_reachable:
                curr_reachable = reachable
                jump_count += 1
            reachable = max(reachable, i + length)
        # [Reader note] Return
        # The final return exposes the result after the traversal and state updates have settled.
        return jump_count

sources.reviewedCode:
# [Reader note] Entry point
# Start from the core routine and keep this mental model in view: Think in terms of how far the current wave of reachable positions can take you before another jump becomes unavoidable.
class Solution:
    def jump(self, nums: List[int]) -> int:
        # [Reader note] State setup
        # This setup stage creates the state needed for the invariant or partial result: Range-based state thinking, measuring progress in layers or intervals, and comparing constructive approaches.
        ans = mx = last = 0
        # [Reader note] Main pass
        # This is the main sweep where the implementation earns its efficiency instead of restarting work from scratch.
        for i, x in enumerate(nums[:-1]):
            mx = max(mx, i + x)
            # [Reader note] Decision point
            # Pay attention here: It is easy to focus on individual jumps when the cleaner viewpoint is to reason about the current reachable band.
            if last == i:
                ans += 1
                last = mx
        # [Reader note] Return
        # The final return exposes the result after the traversal and state updates have settled.
        return ans

writeups.title: jump game ii
writeups.excerpt: ## Prerequisites Before attempting this problem, you should be comfortable with: - **Dynamic Programming (Memoization)** - Caching recursive subproblem results to avoid recomputation - **Dynamic Programming (Tabulation)** - Building solutions bottom-up using iterative DP - **Greedy Algorithms** - Making locally optimal choices that lead to a global optimum - **BFS Level-by-Level Traversal** - Processing nodes in lay...

writeups.title: 45.Jump-Game-II
writeups.excerpt: ### 45.Jump-Game-II 求最少步数到达目的地的算法，最常见的就是BFS。本题可以仿照其思想。 第0步所能到达的点就是0. 第一步所能到的点的右边界就是0+nums[0]，也就是说第一步跨越的范围就是[1, 0+nums[0]。那么第二步所能到达的范围是什么呢？最小的位置，应该是第一步所能达到的最远点的下一个位置；最大的位置，应该是第一步所能达到的所有的点所共同更新的最远边界。 所以我们可以类似BFS的层级遍历，每走一步，弹出旧的元素区间[start,end]，然后装入新的元素区间[start_new, end_new]。 ``` start_new = end+1; end_new = max{i+nums[i]} for i=[start,end] ``` 每一步只要start<=end，就可以认为队列里还有元素，BFS就能继续。直至end_new到达最末的位置，或者start>end。 [Leetcode...
